word_list.word: money (often|) unmakes the men who make it
word_list.pronunciation: mˈʌni ˈɔfən ʌnmˌeɪks ðə mˈɛn hˌuː mˈeɪk ɪt

senses.definition: used to emphasize the negative impacts of wealth on an individual's character and values, as it can lead to greed, selfishness, and a loss of moral values

examples.x: The mentor advised the young entrepreneur that money unmakes the men who make it, and that they should always stay true to their values and principles.

word_list.word: the nearer the church, the (farther|further) from God
word_list.pronunciation: ðə nˈɪɹɚ ðə tʃˈɜːtʃ ðə fˈɑːɹðɚ fˈɜːðɚ fɹʌm ɡˈɑːd

senses.definition: used to suggest that excessive focus on religious institutions or practices can lead to a loss of true spiritual connection or faith

word_list.word: the devil dances in empty pockets
word_list.pronunciation: ðə dˈɛvəl dˈænsᵻz ɪn ˈɛmpti pˈɑːkɪts

senses.definition: used to imply that financial distress or poverty can lead to desperation and immoral behavior

examples.x: "Poverty can be a breeding ground for crime and immorality, as the devil dances in empty pockets, so we should prioritize our poverty crisis," said the social worker during the community outreach program.

word_list.pronunciation: mˈʌk ænd mˈʌni ˈɔfən ɡˌoʊ təɡˈɛðɚ

senses.definition: used to suggest that financial success and wealth often come with a price, and that one may need to engage in unpleasant or unethical activities to achieve it

examples.x: "The entertainment industry is notorious for its shady dealings, and those who want to make it big must be willing to get their hands dirty, as muck and money go together," said the aspiring actor to his friend.

senses.definition: used to emphasize that any wealth or success obtained through dishonest or unethical means is unlikely to last or bring true happiness

examples.x: The CEO of the company was fired after it was discovered that they had been embezzling funds - a harsh lesson that ill-gotten gains never prosper.

word_list.word: where there is muck there is brass
word_list.pronunciation: wˌɛɹ ðɛɹ ɪz mˈʌk ðɛɹ ɪz bɹˈæs

senses.definition: used to imply that there is often money to be made in unpleasant or undesirable tasks or situations

examples.x: The entrepreneur saw an opportunity in the waste management industry, knowing that where there is muck there is brass.

word_list.word: a bribe will enter without knocking
word_list.pronunciation: ɐ bɹˈaɪb wɪl ˈɛntɚ wɪðˌaʊt nˈɑːkɪŋ

senses.definition: used to imply that bribery can allow someone to bypass rules, regulations, and laws that are meant to ensure fairness and accountability

examples.x: Susan witnessed how easily her coworker was persuaded to overlook an ethical violation after receiving an expensive gift, demonstrating that a bribe will enter without knocking.

word_list.word: a thief passes for a gentleman when stealing has made him rich
word_list.pronunciation: ɐ θˈiːf pˈæsᵻz fɚɹə dʒˈɛntəlmən wɛn stˈiːlɪŋ hɐz mˌeɪd hˌɪm ɹˈɪtʃ

senses.definition: used to imply that wealth and success can sometimes mask a person's immoral or criminal behavior, making them appear respectable or honorable
used to imply that people can be influenced by money or incentives, and may compromise their ethics or integrity if the price is high enough

examples.x: Susan saw how people could be persuaded to betray their values and principles for the right incentive, demonstrating that everyone has their price.

senses.definition: used to suggest that money or other gains obtained through dishonest or unethical means are likely to be wasted or used inappropriately
